title: Building Smaller, Faster Images
description:
## Why Image Size Matters

Smaller images pull faster, start faster, and expose less attack surface. A few techniques get you most of the way:

### 1. Start from a small base
`alpine` (~5 MB) or `-slim` variants instead of a full distro. Use `scratch` (empty) for static binaries.

### 2. Multi-stage builds
Compile/build in a heavy stage, then copy **only the artifact** into a tiny final stage:

```dockerfile
FROM golang:alpine AS build
WORKDIR /src
COPY . .
RUN go build -o /app

FROM alpine:latest        # final stage — no compiler, no source
COPY --from=build /app /app
ENTRYPOINT ["/app"]
```

The final image contains the binary but **none** of the build toolchain.

### 3. Minimise layers and cache well
- Combine related `RUN` steps with `&&` and clean up in the **same** layer (e.g. `apt-get install ... && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*`). Cleaning in a later layer doesn't shrink the earlier one.
- Order instructions **least- to most-frequently-changing** so dependency layers stay cached when only source changes.

### 4. Use a `.dockerignore`
Keep `.git`, `node_modules`, build output and secrets out of the build context entirely.

### Inspect what you built
```bash
docker images          # see the final size
docker history <image> # see each layer's contribution
```

Mark complete, then build a multi-stage image in the next task.
